essence: A capital-intensive, infrastructure-like business now cost-competitive with (often cheaper than) fossil power - the structural growth story of energy.
Economics: high upfront capex, near-zero marginal/fuel cost, so returns hinge on capex cost, capacity factor, financing cost, and the PPA tariff.
Long-term contracted (PPA) cash flows make it bond-like - but execution, land/grid access, and tariff aggression create risk.
Intermittency makes storage and grid integration the critical enabler/bottleneck.
Policy, low-cost capital, and module/cell supply chains (China dominance) are decisive; India has huge targets + PLI for local manufacturing.

definition.oneLiner: Companies that generate electricity from renewable sources - solar, wind - and provide the storage and green-fuel technologies enabling the energy transition.
definition.jobToBeDone: Supply clean, increasingly cheap electricity (and green fuels) - decarbonising power while meeting growing energy demand.

valueChain.profitPools: Profit pools sit in low-cost capital + well-sited, high-capacity-factor assets with strong PPAs (the IPP/developer), and increasingly in manufacturing (where protected/subsidised) and in storage/integration; commoditised module manufacturing is brutally price-competitive (China-dominated).
valueChain.powerShift: Value is shifting from subsidy-dependent generation -> cost-competitive, unsubsidised renewables + storage -> integrated firm / round-the-clock clean power and green hydrogen, as costs fall and the grid must absorb intermittency.

variants: IPP / developer (own & operate, PPA cash flows)

unitEconomics.unitOfRevenue: Revenue per unit of electricity (per kWh) under a PPA tariff
unitEconomics.revenueDrivers: Capacity x Capacity-factor. Revenue = installed capacity x capacity (load) factor x PPA tariff; returns hinge on capex cost and cost of capital against that tariff.

costStructure.shape: Almost all cost is upfront capex (equipment + construction) financed with debt; near-zero fuel/marginal cost - so the economics are basically a financing exercise: low capex + cheap capital + high capacity factor vs the PPA tariff.
costStructure.leverage: Once built, operating cost is minimal and every unit generated is near-pure margin against the PPA - so the entire return is decided at financing/construction by capex cost, cost of capital, and resource quality (capacity factor).

moats: Low cost of capital (the biggest edge)
competitionAndMoats.concentration: Generation/IPP is competitive and fragmenting with many developers; module manufacturing is dominated by China; India consolidating around large players (Adani Green, ReNew, Tata Power Renewables) + a manufacturing push.

structuralForces.regulation: Heavily policy-driven (positively) - renewable targets, auctions, RPOs, PLI/manufacturing incentives, and (risk) tariff/contract sanctity and land/grid policy.
structuralForces.cyclicality: Less commodity-cyclical than fossil (contracted cash flows), but exposed to interest-rate cycles (cost of capital is everything) and equipment-price/supply cycles.
structuralForces.substitution: Competes with fossil generation (increasingly winning on cost) and across renewable types; storage vs gas peakers for firming.
structuralForces.supplierBuyerPower: Module/cell suppliers (China) hold power and supply risk; utilities/discoms and corporates (PPA offtakers) wield buyer power; tariff aggression in auctions squeezes developers.

aiPenetration.maturity: Medium-to-high - AI is core to forecasting, asset performance, and grid/storage optimisation.
aiPenetration.valueChainImpact: AI is essential to manage intermittency (forecasting, storage dispatch, curtailment) and to squeeze asset yield/uptime - directly improving the capacity factor and grid value that drive returns.
threatsDisruption: Commoditisation (cheap is the point - thin developer margins)

ranges: LCOE: now at/below fossil in many markets
Cost of capital: the single biggest return driver
PPA tariffs: very low/competitive (India among world's lowest)
Storage costs: falling fast, key to firming
benchmarks.note: DIRECTIONAL - rules of thumb, not audited figures. Verify before quoting.

valueLevers: Lower cost of capital (the biggest lever)

keyTerms.LCOE: Levelised Cost of Energy - lifetime cost per unit; the key competitiveness metric.
keyTerms.Capacity factor / PLF: Actual output vs maximum possible - resource quality.
keyTerms.PPA: Power Purchase Agreement - long-term contracted offtake & price.
keyTerms.Cost of capital: Financing cost - the dominant driver of renewables returns.
keyTerms.Curtailment: Generation wasted when the grid can't absorb it.
keyTerms.Firm / RTC power: Round-the-clock renewable power (with storage) - higher value.
keyTerms.Green hydrogen: Hydrogen made with renewable power - an emerging clean fuel.

questionsToAskFirst: What's the cost of capital vs peers (the key edge)?
Capacity factor / resource quality of the portfolio?
Capex per MW and equipment-supply position?
rookieTraps: Underrating cost of capital as the decisive driver

whatsUniqueHere: Renewables flipped energy economics on its head: there's no fuel cost, so the business isn't really about operations - it's a financing game where whoever has the cheapest capital and best-sited assets wins, because the entire lifetime return is locked in the moment you build. It's the rare infrastructure business that's both the structural growth story of the century and brutally commoditised (cheap is the whole point), with storage and the grid as the make-or-break enablers of its next phase.

indiaLandscape.marketStructure: India is one of the world's fastest-growing renewables markets with ambitious targets (~500 GW non-fossil by 2030): large developers (Adani Green, ReNew, Tata Power Renewables, NTPC Green) scaling solar/wind; record-low tariffs; a strong PLI push to build domestic solar manufacturing and cut China dependence; storage and green hydrogen emerging.
structuralChallenges: China module-supply dependence; tariff aggression squeezing returns
indiaLandscape.note: Semi-perishable - players & policy specifics refresh periodically.

similarTo: Real estate - capital-intensive, financing- and land-driven

currentState.note: PERISHABLE - refresh via research before quoting. Figures approximate.
currentState.marketStructure: India renewables: rapid capacity build toward 2030 targets; record-low solar tariffs; PLI scaling domestic module/cell manufacturing to cut China reliance; storage and round-the-clock/firm-power tenders rising; green hydrogen in early stage; cost of capital and grid/land remain the gating factors.
